Leviticus 7 presents the mechanics of sacrifice with a precision that can feel cold to modern readers. Yet beneath the regulations about fat portions, timing, and priestly shares lies something profoundly unsettling: God is teaching His people what genuine wrongdoing costs. The guilt offering must be made where blood is spilled, the fat burned as an offering to the Lord, because sin is not a private matter to be managed quietly. It belongs to God's attention. The very specificity of these laws—that certain parts must be burned, that others sustain the priests, that timing matters down to whether meat sits overnight—declares that reconciliation with God cannot be casual or improvised. When someone brings a guilt offering, they are not purchasing forgiveness at a discount. They are acknowledging, through careful obedience to these exact requirements, that their violation demands something costly and that God's standards cannot be negotiated.

The peace offering that follows reveals another dimension of the sacrificial system: communion with God and community. A person brings it with gratitude or in fulfillment of a vow, and the offering becomes a shared meal. The worshiper brings the animal with his own hands, waves the breast before the Lord, and then eats the meat with others. This is not distant appeasement of an angry deity—this is fellowship restored, made visible through bread and meat shared. Yet even here, holiness is not soft or permissive. The meat cannot languish until the third day; the person bringing it must be clean; anyone who eats it while unclean is cut off from God's people. The boundary between the sacred and the ordinary is maintained with absolute seriousness.

What moves through this entire chapter is the principle that access to God is conditional and demands reverence. The prohibition against eating fat or blood is not arbitrary dietary restriction but a statement of priority: these belong to the Lord alone (verses 23-27). To consume them anyway is not a minor slip but an offense serious enough to warrant being "cut off" from the community. The repeated insistence that only the clean may participate, that timing must be observed, that portions cannot be altered—all of this asks whether we truly believe that approaching God requires us to become different people. We cannot stumble into His presence unchanged, grab what we want, and leave. We come on His terms, through what He has established, with bodies and hearts that have been made ready.

The final verses anchor all of this in history and promise. This is not a system God imposed thoughtlessly but "the law the Lord gave Moses on Mount Sinai on the day He commanded the Israelites to present their offerings." This is permanent instruction for the generations to come. For those who lived under this system, the sacrifices were their pathway to God—their way of acknowledging sin, receiving cleansing, and returning to fellowship. We read these laws as strangers to this worship, but we cannot read them without seeing how seriously God takes the matter of how His people draw near to Him. Jesus Christ, in His death, fulfilled what these sacrifices pointed toward. He became the guilt offering and the peace offering both. But His fulfillment does not lighten the weight of these chapters; it deepens it. What was rehearsed in blood and fire and precise obedience found its ultimate meaning in His self-offering. To understand what Christ accomplished, we must first feel the gravity of what sin demands and what approach to God requires.
